# Atlantic Tropical Storm Formation – May 2026: NHC Outlook & Current Conditions

## 1. Current NHC Tropical Weather Outlook (as of May 22, 2026)

The **National Hurricane Center** issued its latest Atlantic Tropical Weather Outlook at **8:00 AM EDT, Friday, May 22, 2026** (Forecaster Cangialosi):

> **"For the North Atlantic…Caribbean Sea and the Gulf of America: Tropical cyclone formation is not expected during the next 7 days."**

- **No active tropical disturbances, invest areas, or storms** in the Atlantic basin.
- **No watches or warnings** are in effect.
- The Central Pacific Hurricane Center notes: *"Routine issuance of the Tropical Weather Outlook will resume on June 1, 2026. During the off-season, Special Tropical Weather Outlooks will be issued as conditions warrant."*

## 2. NOAA 2026 Atlantic Hurricane Season Outlook (Issued May 21, 2026)

NOAA/CPC released its official seasonal outlook on **May 21, 2026**, calling for a **below-normal season as the most likely outcome**:

### Probability of Season Category:
| Category | Probability |
|---|---|
| **Below-normal** | **55%** |
| Near-normal | 35% |
| Above-normal | 10% |

### Predicted Ranges (70% probability for each):
| Metric | Forecast Range | 1991–2020 Average |
|---|---|---|
| **Named Storms** | **8–14** | ~14 |
| **Hurricanes** | **3–6** | ~7 |
| **Major Hurricanes** | **1–3** | ~3 |
| **ACE (% of median)** | **45–115%** | 100% |

The predicted ranges are **centered below** the 30-year climatological averages.

## 3. Key Drivers of the 2026 Outlook

### a. El Niño (Primary Suppressive Factor)
- **ENSO Alert Status**: **El Niño Watch** (as of May 14, 2026 CPC ENSO Diagnostic Discussion)
- Current Niño-3.4 index: **+0.4°C** (ENSO-neutral, but warming rapidly)
- Niño-4 index: **+0.5°C**; Niño-1+2 index: **+1.0°C**
- El Niño probability during **May–July 2026: 82%**
- El Niño probability during **Aug–Sept–Oct (ASO) 2026: 98%**
- El Niño probability during **Dec 2026–Feb 2027: 96%**
- **81% chance of moderate or stronger El Niño** during ASO (RONI ≥ 1.0°C)
- Subsurface temperatures have increased for **6 consecutive months**, with widespread significantly above-average subsurface temperatures across the equatorial Pacific
- Westerly wind anomalies observed over the western equatorial Pacific

El Niño generally **suppresses Atlantic hurricane activity** by increasing vertical wind shear over the Main Development Region (MDR).

### b. Atlantic Sea Surface Temperatures (SSTs)
- MDR SST anomaly in **April 2026: +0.2°C** (above average, but much lower than +1.22°C in April 2024)
- North Atlantic basin SST anomalies currently average **+0.27°C**
- Model predictions for ASO SSTs in MDR range from **+0.23°C to +0.59°C**, with multi-model ensemble average of **+0.42°C**
- SSTs are predicted to stay above-normal through at least ASO, consistent with the warm phase of the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation (AMO/AMV)

### c. Trade Winds & Wind Shear
- Trade winds currently **weaker than normal** (locally favorable for storms)
- However, NOAA models predict **above-normal vertical wind shear** over the MDR during ASO, driven by the expected El Niño
- The El Niño-induced shear is expected to **overwhelm** any locally favorable conditions

### d. West African Monsoon
- Upper-level circulation near average
- Monsoon rainfall predicted to be **shifted northward and inland** — not optimal for tropical cyclone development
- African Easterly Jet displaced northward; waves exiting Africa could entrain dry air

## 4. Context: High-Activity Era vs. El Niño

The Atlantic has been in a **high-activity era since 1995**, characterized by warm AMO/AMV conditions. However, the expected El Niño in 2026 is forecast to **counteract** these supportive conditions:

- Historical El Niño years during the high-activity era produced approximately **7–15 named storms, 3–9 hurricanes, 1–4 major hurricanes**
- ACE ranged from **55–165% of median** in such years
- Some El Niño years (e.g., 2002, 2018) still experienced significant activity when Atlantic conditions were very supportive — but such conditions are **not expected in 2026**

## 5. Key Uncertainties

- El Niño could develop **later or weaker** than forecast, which could push activity toward upper ends of ranges
- Local Atlantic conditions (warm SSTs, weak trades) could potentially **overwhelm** remote El Niño influences
- Even in below-normal seasons, **a single storm can cause catastrophic impacts** (e.g., Hurricane Joaquin in the below-normal 2015 season)

## 6. Timeline
- Atlantic hurricane season officially runs **June 1 – November 30, 2026**
- NOAA outlook will be **updated in early August** ahead of the ASO peak
- Next ENSO Diagnostic Discussion scheduled for **June 11, 2026**
